title: Dyeing with comfrey leaves
subtitle: Soft yellow-green from Symphytum officinale leaves on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Common comfrey (Symphytum officinale) is a prolific garden perennial that produces a soft yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool. The leaves are easy to harvest in large quantities, making this a practical and low-cost natural dye for anyone with a garden comfrey plant.
sourceNotes: Comfrey as a natural dye documented in natural dye references and garden craft literature. Symphytum officinale is widely noted as a dye plant in British craft and plant dyeing circles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that bonds between the dye molecule and the wool fibre to fix the colour. Alum gives a clean yellow-green with comfrey.
The extracted plant dye liquid in which the mordanted fibre is placed to absorb colour.
The stability of a dye to washing, light, and abrasion. Comfrey produces a moderately colourfast yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool; it is stable for indoor-use textiles.

content.text: Comfrey is one of the most productive dye plants in a British garden: a single established plant produces large leaves in abundance from spring through autumn. The
is easy to prepare and the
is good for plant-dyed yarn. A standard alum

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots and utensils are dedicated. Wear gloves and apron. Work outdoors or with ventilation. Alum is low-hazard; keep away from food.

content.text: Mordant the wool with alum at 80 degrees Celsius for 45 minutes as standard. The mordanting step is the same as for any plant yellow.

Chop the comfrey leaves roughly and place in the dye-pot with water to cover. Simmer at 80 to 90 degrees Celsius for 45 minutes. The bath will turn a murky yellow-green. Strain through a cloth into a clean pot, pressing the leaves to extract as much liquid as possible.
Add the mordanted, damp fibre to the dye bath. Raise to 80 degrees Celsius and hold for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally. Remove and rinse in progressively cooler water.
The alum mordant bath and comfrey dye bath can be composted or poured down the foul drain in small home quantities. No iron or copper mordants are used.
An iron after-bath shifts the yellow-green toward a deep sage green. Comfrey root, simmered separately for a longer time, produces a tan-pink rather than yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool.

items.problem: The colour is dull and brownish rather than clear yellow-green
items.cause: The leaves were simmered at too high a temperature, breaking down the chlorophyll and shifting the colour toward brown
items.fix: Keep the temperature at or below 90 degrees Celsius throughout the dye extraction. Very high temperatures degrade the flavonoid compounds and produce a muddier result.
